title: Data Collection & Measurement
simple_html:
Once you know who you are studying (your sample), you need a way to get information from them and turn that information into something you can analyse. That is what data collection and measurement are about.

Data collection is the method you use to gather raw information — for example a questionnaire, an interview, an observation, or an existing dataset. Measurement is how you convert what you observe (an opinion, a behaviour, an age) into a value you can record and compare.

Primary vs secondary data

Primary data is collected by you, for your specific question (a survey you run, interviews you conduct). Secondary data already exists — government statistics, company reports, prior datasets — and you reuse it. Primary data fits your question better but costs more time and money.

Levels of measurement

How you measure decides what maths you can later do. The four levels are nominal (labels, e.g. gender), ordinal (ranked order, e.g. "satisfied/neutral/unhappy"), interval (equal gaps, no true zero, e.g. temperature in °C), and ratio (equal gaps with a true zero, e.g. income, age).

Key takeaways

  • Choose your collection method to match your question, not your convenience.
  • Primary data is tailored but expensive; secondary data is cheap but may not fit.
  • The measurement level you choose limits which statistics are valid later — plan analysis before you collect.
  • A well-designed questionnaire avoids leading, double-barrelled, and ambiguous questions.
  • Good measurement is the foundation of reliability and validity (covered next).
intermediate_html:
Data collection and measurement are the engine room of empirical research: the quality of everything downstream — your analysis, findings, and conclusions — is capped by the quality of the data you gather and how well you operationalise your concepts.

Primary versus secondary data

Primary data is original data you collect for your research question (surveys, interviews, experiments, observation). It fits your construct precisely but is costly and time-consuming. Secondary data — published statistics, archival records, organisational databases, prior survey datasets — is fast and cheap, but was collected for someone else's purpose, so it may not capture exactly what you need and you cannot control its quality. A strong proposal justifies the choice against the research question.

Quantitative collection methods

The dominant tool is the questionnaire/survey, delivered self-administered (online, postal) or interviewer-administered (telephone, face-to-face). Structured observation and experiments are also quantitative. The design challenge is operationalisation: turning an abstract construct (e.g. "job satisfaction") into concrete, answerable items.

Qualitative collection methods

Common methods are interviews (structured, semi-structured, unstructured), focus groups, participant observation, and document analysis. These yield rich, contextual data but in volumes that are harder to compare across respondents.

Levels of measurement (Stevens, 1946)

LevelPropertyExampleAllowed stats
NominalCategories, no orderDepartment, genderMode, frequencies
OrdinalRanked, unequal gapsLikert agreementMedian, percentiles
IntervalEqual gaps, no true zeroTemperature °CMean, SD
RatioEqual gaps, true zeroIncome, ageAll, plus ratios

This matters because the measurement level determines which descriptive and inferential statistics are legitimate — you cannot meaningfully average a nominal variable.

Questionnaire design essentials

Use clear, single-idea questions; avoid leading ("Don't you agree that…"), double-barrelled ("fast and friendly?"), and ambiguous wording. Match response formats to the construct — Likert scales for attitudes, ratio scales for quantities. Pilot the instrument to catch confusing items before full rollout.

Worked example

Suppose you study whether remote work raises engagement. You operationalise "engagement" with a validated 9-item scale (each item a 1–7 Likert ordinal/interval-treated response), collect "days remote per week" as ratio data, and "department" as nominal. You pilot with 15 staff, fix two confusing items, then run the survey. How to apply it: decide your analysis first (you plan a correlation), confirm your measurement levels support it, then design items to deliver exactly those variables.
full_html:
Data collection and measurement form the empirical core of the research onion's innermost layers — the "techniques and procedures" through which abstract constructs become analysable evidence (Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019). However sophisticated the philosophy and design, the validity of conclusions is bounded by the fidelity with which concepts are operationalised and data gathered. This lesson covers data sources, collection methods across paradigms, the theory of measurement, instrument design, and the most common pitfalls.

Primary versus secondary data

Primary data is collected first-hand for the specific research question, giving the researcher control over operationalisation, timing, and quality, at the cost of time, money, and access. Secondary data — governmental statistics, industry reports, organisational records, and re-analysed prior datasets — is economical and often longitudinal, but was generated for other purposes, introducing a fit problem (does it measure your construct?) and a quality problem (you did not control its production). Bryman & Bell (2015) note that secondary analysis is undervalued in business research despite high-quality datasets being widely available. A rigorous methods chapter justifies the source against the question rather than defaulting to convenience.

Operationalisation: from construct to variable

The central act of measurement is operationalisation — specifying the observable indicators of an unobservable construct. Following the classic measurement chain, a concept (e.g. "customer loyalty") is defined conceptually, then translated into dimensions, then into concrete indicators (repeat purchase rate, referral behaviour, attitudinal commitment). Poor operationalisation is a primary threat to construct validity (Cronbach & Meehl, 1955) — the degree to which an instrument actually captures the intended concept (see Lesson 7).

Levels of measurement

Stevens' (1946) taxonomy remains foundational. Nominal variables are unordered categories (industry sector); only mode and frequencies are meaningful. Ordinal variables are ranked but with unequal/unknown intervals (rankings, single Likert items); median and non-parametric tests apply. Interval variables have equal intervals but an arbitrary zero (temperature in °C, many summed attitude scales by convention); means and standard deviations are valid. Ratio variables add a true zero (income, age, units sold), permitting ratios and the full range of statistics. A long-running debate concerns whether multi-item Likert scales are ordinal or may be treated as interval: Stevens' strict view says ordinal, but psychometric practice (Carifio & Perla, 2008) argues that summated multi-item scales approximate interval properties and tolerate parametric analysis. The practical lesson: measurement level governs the permissible statistics, so it must be decided before, not after, data collection.

Quantitative collection methods

The workhorse is the survey/questionnaire. Delivery mode (online, postal, telephone, face-to-face) trades cost against response rate and data quality; online surveys are cheap and fast but risk coverage and self-selection bias. Other quantitative methods include structured observation (counting predefined behaviours) and experiments (manipulating an independent variable to establish causality, prized under a positivist philosophy). Each demands a clearly specified, replicable protocol.

Qualitative collection methods

Aligned with interpretivism, qualitative collection emphasises depth and meaning. Interviews range from structured (fixed wording) through semi-structured (an interview guide with probing latitude — the most common business form) to unstructured/narrative. Focus groups exploit group interaction; participant and non-participant observation and ethnography capture behaviour in situ; documentary and archival analysis draws on existing texts. These methods yield rich data but raise challenges of comparability and volume, anticipating the analysis problem in Lesson 8.

Questionnaire and scale design

Sound instruments follow established principles (Dillman, Smyth & Christian, 2014). Each item should be a single, clear idea; avoid leading questions that signal a desired answer, double-barrelled items that ask two things at once, jargon, and negatively-worded confusion. Choose response formats fit for purpose: Likert agreement scales for attitudes, semantic differentials, ranking, or open responses. Established validated scales (e.g. SERVQUAL for service quality) should be reused where they exist, both for validity and comparability. Always pilot the instrument and assess internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) before full deployment.

Worked example

A researcher tests whether weekly remote-work days predict employee engagement. "Engagement" is operationalised via the 9-item UWES scale (1–7 Likert, summed and treated as interval), "remote days" as a ratio count (0–5), and "department" as nominal. A pilot of 15 employees flags two ambiguous items, which are reworded. The full survey (n = 180) achieves Cronbach's alpha = 0.91 for the engagement scale, supporting reliable measurement. Because the planned analysis is a Pearson correlation plus regression, the interval/ratio treatment of the key variables is essential — had engagement been captured as a single ordinal item, only a Spearman correlation would be defensible.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Designing items before deciding the analysis — leaving variables at the wrong measurement level for the intended test.
  • Treating single ordinal items as interval without the multi-item justification.
  • Inventing new scales where validated instruments already exist, sacrificing comparability and validity.
  • Equating a high response count with quality — a large but biased sample is worse than a small representative one.
  • Confusing measurement reliability with validity — a consistent instrument can still measure the wrong thing.

Cross-links

This lesson builds directly on Sampling (Lesson 5), which fixes who supplies the data, and feeds Reliability, Validity & Research Ethics (Lesson 7), where measurement quality is formally assessed, and Data Analysis (Lesson 8), where measurement level dictates which techniques are legitimate. The collection method chosen must also cohere with the philosophy and design set in Lessons 1–4.
